description: Substantive session with a clear, evidence-anchored mindset shift on people-pleasing → self-trust. Expected: ≥1 shift emitted with non-empty evidence_quote, possibly 0–1 breakthroughs (rubric should weigh whether this is shift territory or breakthrough territory), themes like 'people-pleasing' or 'self-trust' marked is_new_theme=true, no trauma flag.

messages.content: Good to see you. What's most alive for you today?
I had a thing with my sister this week. She asked me to host Thanksgiving and I just said yes immediately. Then I spent three days dreading it and being kind of a wreck about it.
Three days dreading it. What was the dread doing — what was it trying to tell you?
I think it was telling me I didn't actually want to do it. But by the time I noticed, I'd already said yes and then it felt impossible to take it back.
There's a gap there — between the yes you said out loud and the yes you actually had inside. What is it like to notice that gap?
Honestly it's kind of devastating. Because I do this all the time. I say yes before I check with myself. And then I resent everyone for things I agreed to.
Mm. Say more about 'before I check with myself.'
It's like there's a part of me whose whole job is making sure no one is disappointed. And it answers the question before the rest of me even hears it. Like an autopilot.
An autopilot whose job is making sure no one is disappointed. What happens to the part of you who would have said something different, if it had been allowed to answer?
She gets… I don't know. Buried. I think I've been ignoring her for so long that I'm not even sure what she would say half the time.
Take a breath. If she could speak right now, about Thanksgiving — what would she say?
Probably that I love my sister and I want to see her, but I don't want to host. I want someone else to host and I want to bring a pie. That's it. That's what I actually want.
What's it like to say that out loud?
It feels like the truth. And also kind of scary because I don't know if I can actually call her and say it.
The truth and the scary live next to each other a lot. What would it mean to trust her — the buried one — even just enough to make the call?
I think it would mean I'm actually allowed to want what I want. Even if it disappoints my sister. Which feels huge to even say.
It is huge. Sit with that for a moment if you can.
Yeah. I want to remember this. That the autopilot isn't me — and the buried voice is closer to who I actually am.
